CZ_NAME: N & NE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / N LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds gusting to 58 mph occurred over northern Lincoln County.  The combination of strong winds and very dry conditions caused areas of blowing dust and dirt to create brownout conditions.  As a result, visibilities were restricted to near zero along sections of Interstate 70.  Along the interstate near Genoa, eight passenger cars and six tractor-trailer trucks were involved in a collision.  Two people were killed and at least 6 others were injured, one seriously.  The multi-vehicle collison forced the closure of the interstate for several hours.

A series of two Pacific storms tracked across the Rocky Mountain region bringing widespread winter conditions to western Colorado. The first system dragged a cold front across the state and spread snowfall across the western Colorado mountains and valleys of northwest Colorado. The second low pressure system dropped down into the Great Basin region and formed a closed low pressure system near the four corner region. This continued mountain snowfall across the central and southern Colorado mountains, and brought significant snow to some of the southern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 15 inches occurred across the area with wind gusts up to 45 mph producing blowing and drifting snow.

A series of two Pacific storms tracked across the Rocky Mountain region bringing widespread winter conditions to western Colorado. The first system dragged a cold front across the state and spread snowfall across the western Colorado mountains and valleys of northwest Colorado. The second low pressure system dropped down into the Great Basin region and formed a closed low pressure system near the four corner region. This continued mountain snowfall across the central and southern Colorado mountains, and brought significant snow to some of the southern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 9 to 12 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist Pacific storm system moved into the Rocky Mountain Region spreading heavy snowfall across the western Colorado and eastern Utah mountains, as well as many of the higher valleys of northern Colorado and Utah. Warm air associated with this system brought a mix of rain and snow to most of the central and southern valleys, with primarily rain falling over southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 3 to 4 inches occurred across about half of the area, including Mancos, Dolores, and Dove Creek, while the rest of the area received 2 inches or less. Wind gusts up to 40 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow. Heavy wet snow weighing down on trees, along with windy conditions caused an 8 hour power outage in and around the town of Dolores, as well as another power outage in the east side of Cortez. Over 1000 customers were affected by the outages. A possible lightning strike is also suspected to be a contributing cause of the outage in Dolores.

Some of the coldest temperatures so far this season followed closely behind this storm system. A few days later, several problems with freezing water pipelines resulted region-wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred across the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from 12 to 19 inches in western portions of the area to 1 to 4 inches in eastern portions. Strong winds gusted up to 40 mph producing blowing and drifting snow and causing localized blizzard conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog frequently reduced visibilities to below one quarter of a mile. Between the Redlands area of Grand Junction and the Grand Junction Regional Airport, visibilities were reported to be between 100 ft. and one quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong, moist, and unseasonably cold winter storm struck the central intermountain region.  Heavy snowfall occurred in most areas across eastern Utah and western Colorado.  Additionally, strong winds in the southern portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado produced blizzard conditions. Some of the coldest temperatures so far this season followed closely behind this storm system. A few days later, several problems with freezing water pipelines resulted region-wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snowfall combined to produce widespread blizzard conditions.  Measured snowfall accumulations in wind protected areas ranged from 12 to 25 inches.  Snow drifts up to 8 feet deep were reported.  US Highway 550 was closed from late on December 7th, with sections of the highway remaining closed through 9:30 AM MST on December 9th.  Chain laws and other travel restrictions were imposed on other roads in the northwest San Juan Mountains.

Western Colorado remained of the back side of a deep low pressure storm system over the central plains states. A cool and moist northerly flow on the back side of this storm brought snowfall to the Gore and Elk mountain ranges of northwest Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist Pacific storm system moved into the Rocky Mountain Region spreading heavy snowfall across the western Colorado and eastern Utah mountains, as well as many of the higher valleys of northern Colorado and Utah. Warm air associated with this system brought a mix of rain and snow to most of the central and southern valleys, with primarily rain falling over southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the area ranged from 9 to 20 inches. Winds gusted up to 60 mph with localized gusts up to 80 mph were reported at the top of Telluride Ski Area as well as Mount Abrahms. These strong winds produced significant blowing and drifting snow with localized blizzard conditions. Travel became very hazardous, if not impossible, which prompted the closure of Highway 550 between Ouray and Red Mountain Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About a foot of snow fell above the 8,000 foot level with lesser amounts below. Wind gusts up to 50 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow with localized blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure area brought six to ten inches of snow across extreme northwest Kansas, southwest Nebraska and eastern Yuma county Colorado.  Winds of 15 to 25 mph produced occasional blowing snow and reduced visibility.  Highest snow totals in Yuma county included 9 inches near Vernon, 6 inches near Wray and Idalia and 8 inches east of Kirk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations included 6 inches at Wray, 9 inches east of Vernon, 6.5 inches at Idalia and 8 inches east of Kirk.  Snowfall in the western part of the county was lighter, including 3 inches at Joes and 4 inches near Clarksville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 12 to 28 inches occurred across the area. Locally higher snowfall amounts were reported, including 31 inches at Crested Butte Ski Area and 33 inches in Gothic. Winds gusted up to 45 mph producing areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist Pacific storm system moved into the Rocky Mountain Region spreading heavy snowfall across the western Colorado and eastern Utah mountains, as well as many of the higher valleys of northern Colorado and Utah. Warm air associated with this system brought a mix of rain and snow to most of the central and southern valleys, with primarily rain falling over southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the area ranged from 12 to 24 inches, with locally higher amounts of up to 33 inches reported on Coal Bank and Molas passes. Strong winds gusted up to 60 mph, with local gusts of up to 76 mph at Mount Putney and Kendall Mountain. These strong winds produced extensive blowing and drifting snow and localized blizzard conditions. Travel over the major mountain passes was restricted with the closure of Highway 550.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient southwest winds developed during the morning and continued through early evening over parts of east central Colorado and northwest Kansas. A wind gust of 61 mph was reported in Cheyenne county with gusts of 45 to 55 mph in Kit Carson and Yuma counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was measured by a mesonet station 9 miles north-northeast of Kit Carson in Cheyenne County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient southwest winds developed during the morning and continued through early evening over parts of east central Colorado and northwest Kansas. A wind gust of 61 mph was reported in Cheyenne county with gusts of 45 to 55 mph in Kit Carson and Yuma counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sharply reduced visibility in blowing dirt resulted in an 8 vehicle accident on Interstate 70 near Stratton, Colorado in Kit Carson County at about 2:25 pm MDT.  Vehicles which were stopped due to poor visibility in blowing dirt were struck from behind by numerous other vehicles.  Five people were treated for varying injuries at the local municipal hospital.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three snowboarders were caught in an avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A party of two snowboarders and one skier were hiking up a slope near Eiseman Hut in the Gore Range when they were caught and buried in an avalanche.  They all came to rest fairly quickly after being carried about 20 vertical feet.  All three were completely buried; the skier and one snowboarder under 1 to 3 feet of snow, while the other snowboarder was under 6 to 7 feet of snow.  The skier was able to dig himself out of the snow after 1 hour and 20 minutes.  The skier then dug out his partners who had been buried under the snow for up to 2 hours and 15 minutes.  All three activated their AvaLungs after they were buried by the snow, and this safety equipment was instrumental in saving their lives.  The three backcountry travelers suffered only minor injuries, though some of their equipment was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow across parts of the Front Range and adjacent plains.  In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  11.5 inches, 1 mile west-northwest of Eldorado Springs; 10 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 9 inches at Genesee, 2 miles southwest of Golden and Ken Caryl; 8 inches, 1 mile south-southeast of Estes Park, Jamestown and White Ranch Open Space; 7 inches, 7 miles north of Glen Haven; 6 inches at Copeland Lake, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland. ||Across the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  9 inches, 3 miles west of Greeley and 2 miles west of Parker; 8 inches at Watkins; 7 inches, 2 miles west-southwest of Byers, 12 miles southwest of Buckley AFB, 2 miles north of Loveland; 6 inches, 1 mile southeast of Fort Collins and 6.5 inches at C.S.U. in Fort Collins and Fort Morgan; 6 inches in Brush, Louviers, 3 miles northeast of Parker and 5 miles south-southeast of Sedalia; 5 inches, 6 miles north-northeast of Woodrow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  11.5 inches, 1 mile west-northwest of Eldorado Springs; 8 inches, 1 mile south-southeast of Estes Park and Jamestown; 7 inches, 7 miles north of Glen Haven; 6 inches at Copeland Lake and 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system generated heavy snow accumulations across portions of southern Colorado.  General amounts in excess of 8 inches occurred across the eastern San Juan...La Garita...Sangre de Cristo...and Wet mountains. Isolated snow amounts of 6 to 8 inches occurred in and around Black Forest and the Air Force Academy (El Paso County).  Widespread snow amounts of 6 to 8 inches included Thatcher...Trinchera...and Kim (Las Animas County) and Sheridan Lake (Kiowa County)...9 to 12 inches at Rye (Pueblo County)...14 inches at Beulah (Pueblo County)...16 inches at Cuchara (Huerfano County)...and finally...20 inches around the summit of Wolf Creek Pass (Mineral County).

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow across parts of the Front Range and adjacent plains.  In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  11.5 inches, 1 mile west-northwest of Eldorado Springs; 10 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 9 inches at Genesee, 2 miles southwest of Golden and Ken Caryl; 8 inches, 1 mile south-southeast of Estes Park, Jamestown and White Ranch Open Space; 7 inches, 7 miles north of Glen Haven; 6 inches at Copeland Lake, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland. ||Across the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  9 inches, 3 miles west of Greeley and 2 miles west of Parker; 8 inches at Watkins; 7 inches, 2 miles west-southwest of Byers, 12 miles southwest of Buckley AFB, 2 miles north of Loveland; 6 inches, 1 mile southeast of Fort Collins and 6.5 inches at C.S.U. in Fort Collins and Fort Morgan; 6 inches in Brush, Louviers, 3 miles northeast of Parker and 5 miles south-southeast of Sedalia; 5 inches, 6 miles north-northeast of Woodrow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  9 inches in Northglenn, 8 inches, 2 miles northeast of Commerce City; 7.5 inches, 7 miles south of Aurora; 6 inches, 1 mile south of Greenwood Village; 5.5 inches at the Denver Water Dept. and 4 inches in Parker.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Peak wind gusts included 93 mph at Red Feather Lakes and 84 mph at the National Wind Technology Center.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, very heavy rain and flash flooding in parts of Morgan County.  In addition, three tornadoes briefly touched down; two in Adams County and the other in Morgan County.  Heavy rain caused extensive flash flooding north of Fort Morgan.  Several county roads were damaged and the heavy rain contributed to one accident.  The combination of large hail, up to ping pong ball size, and heavy rain caused significant crop damage between Weldona and Snyder.  Severe thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of quarters in Adams, Arapahoe, Elbert and Jefferson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from numerous thunderstorms produced extensive flooding of roads and ditches along the Wildcat Creek area.  One resident measured 5 1/2 inches of rainfall with the storms.  Law enforcement reported State Highway 52 and County Road AA were under several feet of water.  Several other county roads were damaged by heavy rain with extensive field flooding reported.  One accident occurred when a driver drove his truck into the washed out portion of County Road Y just east of State Highway 52.  He escaped the submerged truck uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another brief period of high winds occurred in and near the foothills of Jefferson and Boulder Counties. In Nederland, the strong wind snapped a blue spruce which landed on a nearby propane tank. Some roofs in the immediate area were damaged. Power lines were also downed; which left 126 residence without electricity for about 6 hours.  Peak wind gusts included 90 mph at the National Wind Technology Center, and 89 mph, 6 miles northwest of Boulder.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to much of the region as a potent low pressure system dropped out of the Pacific Northwest, and tracked through the Four Corners region and into Southeast Colorado.  As a result, significant snowfall occurred across most of North-Central and Northeast Colorado.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in and near the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide as a deep east to northeasterly upslope flow developed.  ||In the North-Central Mountains storm totals included:  12.5 inches at Williams Park Snotel, 12 inches at Echo Lake, 11.5 inches at Rough and Tumble Snotel, 11 inches at the Deadman Hill, 8.5 inches at Zirkel Snotel and 8 inches at Bear Lake State Park.||In the Front Range Foothills and South Park storm totals included:  24 inches at Eldorado Springs, 21 inches, 2 miles southwest of Golden; 20.5 inches at Genessee, 18 inches, 3 mile southeast of Pinecliffe; 16 inches, 5 miles west-southwest of Hartsel and Stontia Springs Dam; 15 inches at Roxborough State Park, 14.5 inches at Nederland and 6 miles southwest of Evergreen, 14 inches, 3 miles southwest of Conifer; 13 inches at Evergreen, 12 inches at Fairplay and 3 miles southeast of Indian Hills; 11.5 inches at Gross Reservoir.

The heaviest snowfall occurred in and near the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide as a deep east to northeasterly upslope flow developed.  ||In the North-Central Mountains storm totals included:  12.5 inches at Williams Park Snotel, 12 inches at Echo Lake, 11.5 inches at Rough and Tumble Snotel, 11 inches at the Deadman Hill, 8.5 inches at Zirkel Snotel and 8 inches at Bear Lake State Park.||In the Front Range Foothills and South Park storm totals included:  24 inches at Eldorado Springs, 21 inches, 2 miles southwest of Golden; 20.5 inches at Genessee, 18 inches, 3 mile southeast of Pinecliffe; 16 inches, 5 miles west-southwest of Hartsel and Stontia Springs Dam; 15 inches at Roxborough State Park, 14.5 inches at Nederland and 6 miles southwest of Evergreen, 14 inches, 3 miles southwest of Conifer; 13 inches at Evergreen, 12 inches at Fairplay and 3 miles southeast of Indian Hills; 11.5 inches at Gross Reservoir.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second potent spring storm of the season brought very heavy snow to locations in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Deep easterly upslope conditions developed east of the Continental Divide as a slow moving storm system moved across southern Colorado.  As a result, snowfall piled up to nearly 5 feet in parts of the foothills.  The heavy snow forced the closure of an 80-mile section of Interstate 70 stretching from Golden to Vail.  The Red Cross opened up several shelters in Silverthorne and Frisco for stranded travelers.  The interstate was closed for approximately 16 hours.  Two avalanches over Berthoud Pass forced the closure of U.S. Highway 40.  Two vehicles and a snow plow were caught in the second slide but no injuries were reported.  The heavy snow snapped power lines in Evergreen and Nederland causing outages which affected 14,200 residents.||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  56 inches, 3 miles south of Rollinsville; 54 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 48 inches near Buckhorn Mountain; 43 inches at Aspen Springs, 42 inches at Evergreen, 38 inches, 3 miles west of Conifer; 37 inches at St. Mary's Glacier, 34 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 33 inches at Genesee; 32 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Evergreen.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 1 to 2 1/2 feet.||In the North Central Mountains storm totals included:  35 inches at Bear Lake State Park, 34 inches at Lake Eldora, 28 inches near Tabernash, 25 inches at Willow Park, 23 inches at Lake Irene, 21 inches at Jones Pass.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 10 to 20 inches.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging downslope winds were responsible for triggering two wildfires, that threatened the city of Boulder.  Wind gusts ranging from 75 to 107 mph downed power lines, which ignited the wildfires.  Although the fires never merged; they were close enough that firefighters were able to build a perimeter around both of them.  The fires quickly scorched 3,000 acres and forced the evacuation of up to 1,400 families.  One home was destroyed along with several barns and outbuildings.  Three firemen suffered minor injuries.  Damaging winds occurred in the foothills of Jefferson and Park Counties as well.  In Bailey, power lines were downed by falling trees.  A tin roof on an auto repair shop in town was almost completely blown off. ||Peak wind gusts included:  107 mph, 3 miles south of Mt. Audubon; 101 mph atop Berthoud Pass, 92 mph, 3 miles south of Evergreen; 87 mph, 6 miles northwest of Boulder; 85 mph at Carter Lake; 81 mph, 2 miles east-northeast of Bergen Park and at the National Wind Technology Center; 79 mph, 4 miles northeast of Nederland; 77 mph, 3 miles west of Sheridan; 75 mph, 4 miles south of Ft. Collins and at Genesee.

As the storm moved into the Northeast Plains the winds strengthened and produced blizzard conditions.  Most of the Urban Corridor was spared from the heavy snow.  The exception occurred from Fort Collins north to the Wyoming Border.  Heavy snow and strong winds forced the closure of State Highway 287 and Interstate 25 due to poor visibilities and multiple accidents.  ||Further east, the blizzard conditions became widespread.  Storm totals generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches.  The combination of snow and very strong northerly winds gusting to over 60 mph produced the blizzard.  Snowpacked roads and zero visibilities were reported, as trained spotters observed snow drifts from 2 to 5 feet in depth.  The combination of strong wind and heavy snow snapped 14 power poles along U.S. Highway 36 near Strasburg.  In Arapahoe County, 8 poles were lost between County Roads 38 and 193 near Deer Trail, while 7 poles snapped in Bennett.||Numerous road closures were reported including:  Interstate 70 was in both directions from Denver to the Kansas stateline; Interstate 76 in both directions from Fort Morgan to the Nebraska state line; Colorado Highway 71, from Limon to Brush; Colorado Highway 71, between Colorado Highway 14 and the Nebraska State Line; and U.S. Highway 85 from Ault to the Wyoming Border.  The Red Cross operated an overnight shelter in Fort Morgan High School for more than 40 stranded travelers.  The strong winds and snow caused numerous power outages in Weld County as well, which affected approximately 8,600 Xcel customers.  The outages in Deer Trail affected 1,700 customers.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust blew a trailer in the path of two vehicles along State Highway 36, between Boulder and Lyons.  The two vehicles were in the northbound lane when a wind gust blew a fifth-wheel trailer into their path, as it approached from the opposite lane.  The trailer left the road surface, and collided with the top left side of the first  car, causing extensive damage.  A van following the first vehicle then struck the trailer.  The driver of the van suffered facial injuries.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist spring storm produced heavy snow over part of the Urban Corridor and a prolonged period of light to moderate rain over the rest of the area.  The snow level hovered around 5500 feet for most of the event.  The heavy wet snow also knocked out power to thousands residents.  In Denver, 2200 customers were without power, and in Boulder, 2700 customers were affected by the outages.   ||The heaviest snowfall was reported across the western and southern suburbs of Denver, and further south along the Palmer Divide.  Storm totals included:  22 inches, 8.4 miles south of Franktown; 18 inches, 10 miles south-southeast of Buckley Air Force Base; 17 inches near Cherry Creek and 7 miles south of Sedalia; 16 inches, 6.5 miles southwest of Castle Rock; 15 inches near Beverly Hills; 12 inches, 2 miles southeast of Highlands Ranch and at Lafayette; with 11 inches in Broomfield.  Elsewhere storm totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches.  For those areas the received very little snow, rainfall totals generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At Denver International Airport, hundreds of flights were canceled.  In addition, schools throughout the region were shut down and many roads closed due to multiple accidents.  Dozens of vehicles slid of Interstate 25 which involved up to 75 vehicles, between Fort Collins and Cheyenne.  Fifteen people were treated for minor injuries.  Portions of U.S. Highway 36, between Denver and Boulder, were also closed during the day.  The Red Cross opened up six shelters for stranded motorists.||In the Urban Corridor and Palmer Divide areas, storm totals included:  18 inches in Boulder, 15 inches at Lafayette, 14 inches in Broomfield, 2 miles northwest of Elizabeth and 5 miles north of Thornton; 13.5 inches, 14 miles west of Agate; 12.5 inches at Highlands Ranch, 12 inches, 3 miles northeast of Castle Rock and Lakewood; 11.5 inches, 2 miles northeast of Commerce City; 11 inches in Greeley and 5 miles south-southeast of Sedalia,  10.5 inches, 2 miles west of Arapahoe Park; 10 inches in Littleton and 2 miles north of Loveland; and 8 inches, 2 miles west-southwest of Byers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped 2-5 inches of snow, and strong northerly winds of 30-50 mph resulted in blizzard conditions across extreme eastern Colorado, northwest Kansas and parts of southwest Nebraska.  Interstate 70 in Colorado was closed for approximately 6 hours due to occasional whiteout conditions from the early evening hours on April 4th to early morning on the 5th.  Snowfall accompanying the storm in Colorado included:  Yuma county...T-3.5, Kit Carson County...T-2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported whiteout conditions in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the storm moved into the Northeast Plains the winds strengthened and produced blizzard conditions.  Most of the Urban Corridor was spared from the heavy snow.  The exception occurred from Fort Collins north to the Wyoming Border.  Heavy snow and strong winds forced the closure of State Highway 287 and Interstate 25 due to poor visibilities and multiple accidents.  ||Further east, the blizzard conditions became widespread.  Storm totals generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches.  The combination of snow and very strong northerly winds gusting to over 60 mph produced the blizzard.  Snowpacked roads and zero visibilities were reported, as trained spotters observed snow drifts from 2 to 5 feet in depth.  The combination of strong wind and heavy snow snapped 14 power poles along U.S. Highway 36 near Strasburg.  In Arapahoe County, 8 poles were lost between County Roads 38 and 193 near Deer Trail, while 7 poles snapped in Bennett.||Numerous road closures were reported including:  Interstate 70 was in both directions from Denver to the Kansas stateline; Interstate 76 in both directions from Fort Morgan to the Nebraska state line; Colorado Highway 71, from Limon to Brush; Colorado Highway 71, between Colorado Highway 14 and the Nebraska State Line; and U.S. Highway 85 from Ault to the Wyoming Border.  The Red Cross operated an overnight shelter in Fort Morgan High School for more than 40 stranded travelers.  The strong winds and snow caused numerous power outages in Weld County as well, which affected approximately 8,600 Xcel customers.  The outages in Deer Trail affected 1,700 customers.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow, induced by a strong upper level jetstream, wreaked havoc on the morning commute in Denver, as well as area north and east of the Denver area. The snow was heaviest on the east side of the Denver metro area where storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches.  The combination of reduced visibilities and snowpacked roadways resulted in multiple accidents during the morning rush hour including an 18-car pileup, a school bus crash and at least three fatalities.  In southern Weld County, several crashes were reported along Interstate 76 near Hudson, and U.S. Highway 85 near Platteville.  In Adams County, numerous accidents also occurred along Interstate 70 near Watkins.

A major winter storm tracked across the Four Corners area and through northern New Mexico bringing heavy snow and strong winds to all of southern Colorado.  Blizzard conditions occurred across nearly all of the southeast plains.  In the Pikes Peak Region...around a foot of snow fell in the northern part of El Paso County...while around 5 inches fell in the central and southern sections of the county.  Snow amounts across the rest of the I-25 corridor ranged from around 8 inches in Pueblo to 16 inches in Trinidad (Las Animas County).  Snow amounts across the rest of the southeast plains ranged from 16 to 20 inches.  Snow amounts were in excess of 8 inches across the mountains and high valleys.

Several roads...including US Highway 50 between Pueblo and Penrose...and a section of I-25 around Walsenburg...were closed for several hours.  Power was temporarily knocked out to around 14,000 customers...and numerous airline flights were cancelled at Colorado Springs.  In the Pikes Peak Region...around a foot of snow fell in the northern part of El Paso County...while around 5 inches fell in the central and southern sections of the county.  Snow amounts across the rest of the I-25 corridor ranged from around 8 inches in Pueblo to 16 inches in Trinidad (Las Animas County).  Snow amounts across the rest of the southeast plains ranged from 16 to 20 inches.
A small tornado touched down in southern Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado did not produce any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow, induced by a strong upper level jetstream, wreaked havoc on the morning commute in Denver, as well as area north and east of the Denver area. The snow was heaviest on the east side of the Denver metro area where storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches.  The combination of reduced visibilities and snowpacked roadways resulted in multiple accidents during the morning rush hour including an 18-car pileup, a school bus crash and at least three fatalities.  In southern Weld County, several crashes were reported along Interstate 76 near Hudson, and U.S. Highway 85 near Platteville.  In Adams County, numerous accidents also occurred along Interstate 70 near Watkins.

A severe thunderstorm produced large hail, up to 1.50 inches in diameter, southeast of Julesburg.

Strong gradient winds developed behind a cold front, resulting in numerous wind gusts of 45-65 mph across eastern Colorado and extreme western Kansas.  Mesonet and ASOS stations reported a maximum wind gust of 63 mph in Yuma County, 63 mph in Kit Carson County, 62 mph in Cheyenne County, and 59 mph in Sherman County KS.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second potent spring storm of the season brought very heavy snow to locations in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Deep easterly upslope conditions developed east of the Continental Divide as a slow moving storm system moved across southern Colorado.  As a result, snowfall piled up to nearly 5 feet in parts of the foothills.  The heavy snow forced the closure of an 80-mile section of Interstate 70 stretching from Golden to Vail.  The Red Cross opened up several shelters in Silverthorne and Frisco for stranded travelers.  The interstate was closed for approximately 16 hours.  Two avalanches over Berthoud Pass forced the closure of U.S. Highway 40.  Two vehicles and a snow plow were caught in the second slide but no injuries were reported.  The heavy snow snapped power lines in Evergreen and Nederland causing outages which affected 14,200 residents.||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  56 inches, 3 miles south of Rollinsville; 54 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 48 inches near Buckhorn Mountain; 43 inches at Aspen Springs, 42 inches at Evergreen, 38 inches, 3 miles west of Conifer; 37 inches at St. Mary's Glacier, 34 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 33 inches at Genesee; 32 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Evergreen.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 1 to 2 1/2 feet.||In the North Central Mountains storm totals included:  35 inches at Bear Lake State Park, 34 inches at Lake Eldora, 28 inches near Tabernash, 25 inches at Willow Park, 23 inches at Lake Irene, 21 inches at Jones Pass.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 10 to 20 inches.

An unusual overnight wind event produced gusts of 70-100 mph over Yuma County when light rain showers channeled strong winds aloft to the surface, resulting in widespread damage.  Many utility poles were down, and outbuildings and roofs were damaged or destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather stations near the town of Yuma reported wind gusts of 66, 74 and 98 mph around midnight.  Many utility poles were reported down in the county, especially along US 385 and CR 26, tree limbs 5-6 inches in diameter were broken, part of a roof was blown off a building, signs and awnings were damaged, and outbuildings were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Spring storm generated heavy snow over portions of southern Colorado. An intense Spring storm system produced heavy snow over a large portion of southern Colorado.  Some of the heavier snow amounts include 6 and more inches in and around Rye (Pueblo County), locally, 8 inches in and around Alamosa, the Great Sand Dunes and the Black Forest (El Paso County), and 10 inches in Rosita (Custer County) and Beulah (Pueblo County).  In addition...around a foot of snow fell across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and surrounding high valleys.  Crestone (Saguache County)...on the east side of the San Luis Valley...received up to 23 inches of snow.

An early season spring storm dropped southeastward from the Pacific northwest into the Great Basin region. This storm spread snowfall from north to south across the mountains and higher valleys of western Colorado. The heaviest snow and strong winds occurred with a cold front that passed from north to south across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event continued through April 2. Heavy snowfall of 9 to 15 inches occurred with strong winds that gusted up to 30 to 55 mph. For a continuation of this event please see the April 2009 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season spring storm dropped out of the Pacific Northwest and into the Great Basin bringing heavy snowfall and strong gusty winds to the mountains of western Colorado, as well as some of the higher valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranging from 3 to 5 inches occurred with winds that gusted up to 20 to 30 mph.  See the March 2009 Storm Data Publication for the beginning of this event.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring storm moved into western Colorado and then tracked eastward along the northern Colorado border. Moisture wrapping around the backside of this storm system brought significant snowfall to the northern and east central mountains of Western Colorado. Strong winds on the backside of the storm also generated blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches occurred while winds gusted up to 15 to 25 mph causing blowing and drifting snow.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon winds sparked a wildfire from a controlled burn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A controlled burn of cornfield stubble was blown out of control by strong winds in the afternoon and evening hours. This caused a wildfire that charred 300 acres of land. The fire was near Jay Jay and South River roads near the city of Montrose. The dense billowing smoke and close proximity of the fire to Jay Jay Road caused a threat to traffic and the closure of the road until the fire was maintained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds whipped a small fire being used to burn weeds into a raging wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Grand Junction home owner in the Redlands area of the city was burning a brush pile when the fire got out of control due to strong winds that developed.  The wildfire burned through 40 acres of residential and open space, forcing the evacuation of 20 homes in the Redlands area of Grand Junction. One firefighter attending to the fire sustained minor burns to his face.  Some monitoring gauges and plastic coverings on the outside of one of the fire engines were melted as the fire roared across Highway 340.  The exterior wall of one house was charred, and landscaping was destroyed on several other properties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail in Larimer and Weld Counties.  In Weld County, two weak tornadoes were also reported.  Hail up to golfball size caused heavy crop damage north and east of Greeley, where fields of sugar, beets, pinto beans, corn and carrots were damaged.   The combination of heavy rain and hail shut down several county roads around Greeley, as hail piled in small drifts and overflowed ditches.  Lightning strikes caused damage in Boulder, Superior and at Front Range Airport near Watkins.

Strong winds across the Grand Valley caused a campfire near the Colorado River to ignite a destructive wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A campfire that was started on an island near the confluence of the Colorado and Gunnison rivers burned out of control when strong winds blew across the Grand Valley during the afternoon and evening hours. Around 6:30 pm, the wildfire jumped the Colorado River moving across a bike path and railroad tracks near the southbound off-ramp of the Riverside Parkway. The fire moved into a salvage yard and burned down a building used for office space and storage. Mobile home residents near Riverside Parkway were evacuated for about 3 hours.

A cold airmass in place over Western Colorado, along with clearing skies and light winds overnight, allowed low temperatures in some of the lower valleys to drop below freezing during the early part of the growing season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped to between 23 and 25 degrees across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced quarter size hail across Adams, Denver, Douglas and Elbert Counties.  In addition, wet microbursts produced thunderstorm outflow winds up to 80 mph across Logan, Morgan, Phillips and Sedgwick Counties.  Two weak tornadoes were reported in Elbert and Logan Counties.  The severe weather caused a fatal automobile accident along Interstate 76 between Sterling and Julesburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed branches two to three inches in diameter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst contributed to a fatal car accident near Holyoke.  The combination of very strong winds and heavy rain caused extremely poor visibilities.  The severe thunderstorms wind rolled a semi truck hauling an empty trailer on its side.  The trailer came to rest on the center median while the truck ended up in the left eastbound lane.   A van following behind then struck the undercarriage of the rolled over semi truck and ran off the road.  The driver the of the van was killed.  His teenage son and the driver of the truck suffered minor injuries.

An early season spring storm dropped out of the Pacific Northwest and into the Great Basin bringing heavy snowfall and strong gusty winds to the mountains of western Colorado, as well as some of the higher valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred with amounts ranging from 5 to 12 inches. Strong winds also gusted up to 30 to 55 mph with locally stronger gusts, including 70 mph at Eagle Mountain.  See the March 2009 Storm Data Publication for the beginning of this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were injured, one seriously, when they were struck by lightning while skeet shooting.  The more seriously injured man was burned when the lightning struck his shotgun, causing it to explode in his hands.  The combination of heavy rain and hail also caused flash flooding in Central Weld County.  Flash flooding was reported over County Road 24 and Two Rivers Parkway near Johnstown.  The water was over 12 inches deep in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skiers triggered an avalanche while skiing near Telluride.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A party of skiers triggered an avalanche while skiing in Bear Creek, near the Telluride ski area. The avalanche carried one skier 1400 vertical feet over several cliff bands, to the valley floor. His entire body was buried except for one hand. He was rescued about 7 minutes later by other members in his party, and was carried out by the Telluride Helitrax and Telluride Ski Patrol. The victim suffered multible broken bones and was taken to St. Mary's Hospital in Grand Junction.

Severe weather again broke out across Northeast Colorado.  Three tornadoes touched down in southwest Elbert County; the strongest was rated EF2.  In addition to the tornadoes, severe thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of golfballs.  Damaging thunderstorm winds also downed a tree in Boulder.  Other counties affected by severe thunderstorms included:  Boulder, Douglas, Larimer, Lincoln and Sedgwick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began 8 miles south-southwest of Elizabeth and produced minor damage to two homes.  The tornado then moved southeast and intensified with EF1 and EF2 damage observed 9 miles south of Elizabeth, with substantial damage to a farm, trees, and snapped power poles.   The tornado moved on a more southerly track for the next 2 miles with additional EF1 and EF2 damage 11 miles south of Elizabeth.  The tornado leveled a 70-yr old barn and a nearby cinder-block shed was torn open.  Major tree damage was also observed.   The tornado then continued southward for another mile before lifting with some EF0 and EF1 damage 12 miles south of Elizabeth.  Minor damage to another home was observed with several large trees uprooted.

Large damaging hail fell in eastern Colorado, breaking windows in homes and piling hail into two foot drifts on the north side of homes.  When the storm crossed Interstate 70 just east of Burlington, Colorado, numerous cars received broken windows and windshields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four golfers were struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four men were golfing on the seventh green at the Cedar Ridges Golf Course in Rangely when a tree 15 to 20 yards away was struck by lightning.  The charge from the lightning bolt traveled from the tree through the ground and struck the four golfers, blowing the golf clubs out of their hands and leaving them stunned for a period of time.  They heard popping noises on the tops of their heads as the charge exited through their caps.  One of the golfers felt like the hair on his head was on fire.  The tree struck by lightning was split open and a large amount of bark was blown off.  Information in this report was provided by one of the lightning strike victims.

Severe thunderstorms in Denver and the surrounding metropolitan area produced five tornadoes, large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.  The strongest of the tornadoes touched down near Southlands Mall, in southeast Aurora.  Two men were injured, one seriously.   Overall the damage was rated mild to moderate, which translated to an EF1 rating.  Four other tornadoes touched down, two of which caused minor damage.  Another EF1 tornado damaged a home, several outbuildings, and injured some horses in unincorporated Elbert County.||In addition to the tornadoes, large hail from 1 to 3 inches in diameter was observed.  In parts of Aurora and Centennial, thunderstorm winds blew down power lines and caused electrical outages.  Approximately 8,000 homes and businesses were left without power for nearly two hours.  Most of the damage consisted of broken windows and roofs.  This day was the first in a series of eight to cause damage along the Urban Corridor.  Damage to homes and property along the Front Range totalled $161 million during the 8-day span, making it the state's fifth highest insurance loss.  The majority of the property damage was caused by hail; 21,000 automobile claims and 13,000 homeowner claims were filed.

A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds, large hail and very heavy rain across the western and southern suburbs of Denver.  Widespread damage was observed in the cities of Arvada, Wheat Ridge and Lakewood.  The intense straightline winds were the result of a wet microburst which downed hundreds of trees and snapped power poles.  Winds gusts to 80 mph were reported along with nickel to golfball size hail.  The combination of wind and hail produced widepread damage to homes and vehicles.  The damage claim estimates totaled $350 million; about 32,900 claims were filed for homes, while another 19,500 claims were for vehicles.  As many as 90,000 homes and businesses were left without power.  In some areas it took up to 3 days to completely restore electrical service.  Minor injuries were reported from broken glass during the storm, but no one was hospitalized.   The storm crippled the computers at the Colorado Crime Information Center in Lakewood for nearly 3 hours.  One weak tornado was also observed.||In Larimer and Weld Counties, severe thunderstorms produced widepread damage over approximately 150 square miles of farmland.  In all, 400 farms were directly impacted by severe weather.  Damage to crops alone was estimated to be around $4 million; most of the damage was observed in Weld County.

Severe thunderstorms in Denver and the surrounding metropolitan area produced five tornadoes, large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds. A long-lasting supercell tracked across southeast Colorado, producing large hail, high winds, and two short-lived tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed in Crowley County at 1735 MST, 8.1 miles east-southeast of Sugar City and moved into Otero County 8.9 miles east-southeast of Sugar City, or  6.5 miles north of Cheraw at 1736 MST. The tornado traveled another 0.35 miles to the east and dissipated at 1737 MST, 9.25 miles east-southeast of Sugar City, or 6.4 miles north of Cheraw.

Severe thunderstorms in Denver and the surrounding metropolitan area produced five tornadoes, large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.  The strongest of the tornadoes touched down near Southlands Mall, in southeast Aurora.  Two men were injured, one seriously.   Overall the damage was rated mild to moderate, which translated to an EF1 rating.  Four other tornadoes touched down, two of which caused minor damage.  Another EF1 tornado damaged a home, several outbuildings, and injured some horses in unincorporated Elbert County.||In addition to the tornadoes, large hail from 1 to 3 inches in diameter was observed.  In parts of Aurora and Centennial, thunderstorm winds blew down power lines and caused electrical outages.  Approximately 8,000 homes and businesses were left without power for nearly two hours.  Most of the damage consisted of broken windows and roofs.  This day was the first in a series of eight to cause damage along the Urban Corridor.  Damage to homes and property along the Front Range totalled $161 million during the 8-day span, making it the state's fifth highest insurance loss.  The majority of the property damage was caused by hail; 21,000 automobile claims and 13,000 homeowner claims were filed.

A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds, large hail and very heavy rain across the western and southern suburbs of Denver.  Widespread damage was observed in the cities of Arvada, Wheat Ridge and Lakewood.  The intense straightline winds were the result of a wet microburst which downed hundreds of trees and snapped power poles.  Winds gusts to 80 mph were reported along with nickel to golfball size hail.  The combination of wind and hail produced widepread damage to homes and vehicles.  The damage claim estimates totaled $350 million; about 32,900 claims were filed for homes, while another 19,500 claims were for vehicles.  As many as 90,000 homes and businesses were left without power.  In some areas it took up to 3 days to completely restore electrical service.  Minor injuries were reported from broken glass during the storm, but no one was hospitalized.   The storm crippled the computers at the Colorado Crime Information Center in Lakewood for nearly 3 hours.  One weak tornado was also observed.||In Larimer and Weld Counties, severe thunderstorms produced widepread damage over approximately 150 square miles of farmland.  In all, 400 farms were directly impacted by severe weather.  Damage to crops alone was estimated to be around $4 million; most of the damage was observed in Weld County.

A strong low pressure system moved into the Great Basin region creating a strong southwesterly flow aloft over the area. Strong and gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the associated surface cold front that pushed through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the area ranged from 50 to 61 mph. Some tree branches were broken and loose residential items were blown around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Great Basin region creating a strong southwesterly flow aloft over the area. Strong and gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the associated surface cold front that pushed through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusted up to 49 mph across the area. Winds blew a tree down onto a home in the town of Gunnison, and also caused a short power outage in the Dos Rios area of town. Several tree branches were also blown down.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Great Basin region creating a strong southwesterly flow aloft over the area. Strong and gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the associated surface cold front that pushed through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the area ranged from 50 to 65 mph with local gusts of up to 70 mph at the Jay RAWS site.  Trees were blown down by the wind and blocked the road leading to the Storm King communications site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Great Basin region creating a strong southwesterly flow aloft over the area. Strong and gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the associated surface cold front that pushed through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the area ranged from 50 to 60 mph with local gusts of up to 78 mph near the community of Whitewater. Some tree branches were broken and loose residential items were tossed around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a severe thunderstorm downed power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorm winds knocked two power poles down at the intersection of County Roads 33 and 98.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and hail in the Hayman burn area triggered several mudslides and caused some minor flooding.

A strong cold front moved into the region and brought much colder air across the area. This, combined with decreasing winds overnight, allowed minimum temperatures to drop below freezing for many of the valleys across western Colorado. This brought the first widespread freeze for these locations, which effectively ended the growing season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures dropped to between 24 and 27 degrees across the area.

The intense straightline winds were the result of a wet microburst which downed hundreds of trees and snapped power poles.  Winds gusts to 80 mph were reported along with nickel to golfball size hail.  The combination of wind and hail produced widepread damage to homes and vehicles.  The damage claim estimates totaled $350 million; about 32,900 claims were filed for homes, while another 19,500 claims were for vehicles.  As many as 90,000 homes and businesses were left without power.  In some areas it took up to 3 days to completely restore electrical service.  Minor injuries were reported from broken glass during the storm, but no one was hospitalized.   The storm crippled the computers at the Colorado Crime Information Center in Lakewood for nearly 3 hours.  One weak tornado was also observed.||In Larimer and Weld Counties, severe thunderstorms produced widepread damage over approximately 150 square miles of farmland.  In all, 400 farms were directly impacted by severe weather.  Damage to crops alone was estimated to be around $4 million; most of the damage was observed in Weld County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down but did no damage.

A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds, large hail and very heavy rain across the western and southern suburbs of Denver.  Widespread damage was observed in the cities of Arvada, Wheat Ridge and Lakewood.  The intense straightline winds were the result of a wet microburst which downed hundreds of trees and snapped power poles.  Winds gusts to 80 mph were reported along with nickel to golfball size hail.  The combination of wind and hail produced widepread damage to homes and vehicles.  The damage claim estimates totaled $350 million; about 32,900 claims were filed for homes, while another 19,500 claims were for vehicles.  As many as 90,000 homes and businesses were left without power.  In some areas it took up to 3 days to completely restore electrical service.  Minor injuries were reported from broken glass during the storm, but no one was hospitalized.   The storm crippled the computers at the Colorado Crime Information Center in Lakewood for nearly 3 hours.  One weak tornado was also observed.||In Larimer and Weld Counties, severe thunderstorms produced widepread damage over approximately 150 square miles of farmland.  In all, 400 farms were directly impacted by severe weather.  Damage to crops alone was estimated to be around $4 million; most of the damage was observed in Weld County.
Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Hayman burn area.  A severe thunderstorm produced ping pong size hail south of Castle Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced mudslides in the Hayman burn area.  Trees, stumps, sticks,  debris, and decomposed granite came down with the mudslides.   Most of the damage occurred from Six Mile Creek to Forest Service Road 211 above the Wigwam Fishing Club.   Road crews had to totally restore shoulders and slopes and cleaned out ditches downstream of draws and ravines.  The mudslides washed out a 250-ft stretch of one shoulder of State Highway 126, near the turnoff to Cheesman Reservoir, and a large section of guard rail was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Great Basin and Four Corners area. A strong and moist southwest flow ahead of the system followed by much colder air behind a cold front resulted in heavy snow for the mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 10 to 23 inches occurred while wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter pummeled a forested area southeast of Steamboat Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms in Denver and the surrounding metropolitan area produced five tornadoes, large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.  The strongest of the tornadoes touched down near Southlands Mall, in southeast Aurora.  Two men were injured, one seriously.   Overall the damage was rated mild to moderate, which translated to an EF1 rating.  Four other tornadoes touched down, two of which caused minor damage.  Another EF1 tornado damaged a home, several outbuildings, and injured some horses in unincorporated Elbert County.||In addition to the tornadoes, large hail from 1 to 3 inches in diameter was observed.  In parts of Aurora and Centennial, thunderstorm winds blew down power lines and caused electrical outages.  Approximately 8,000 homes and businesses were left without power for nearly two hours.  Most of the damage consisted of broken windows and roofs.  This day was the first in a series of eight to cause damage along the Urban Corridor.  Damage to homes and property along the Front Range totalled $161 million during the 8-day span, making it the state's fifth highest insurance loss.  The majority of the property damage was caused by hail; 21,000 automobile claims and 13,000 homeowner claims were filed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched and caused minor damage.  Several trees were downed in an open field.  The tornado also damaged a metal fence and minor roof damage to a nearby residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and a series of upper level disturbances brought a prolonged period of snow and strong winds to much of western Colorado. This resulted in widespread heavy snow across the mountains and some of the lower valleys. Additionally, cold air trapped in the valleys resulted in areas of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around 12 inches occurred above 8000 feet while wind gusts of 30 to 55 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in southeast Aurora, south of Buckley Airport, and moved south-southeast across open fields, some residential areas, and Southlands Mall.  Numerous homes and apartments suffered window damage, minor roof damage and there were a few broken garage doors.  The Southlands Mall had some roof and window damage, and wall damage to one of its restaurants.  South of the mall, Lowe's received some roof damage, and lightweight sheds and other structures were destroyed.  One resident, in the Sorrel Ranch subdivision, north of the mall, was swept off his porch while attempting to get close-up pictures of the tornado.  He was found unconcious and bleeding in his front yard, with a dislocated shoulder, an injured neck, and multiple other injuries.  In addition, the tornado flipped a trailer home.  A man inside the trailer suffered minor injuries.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold Pacific storm system dropped down into the Great Basin and then tracked through the Rocky Mountain region, pushing a strong cold front that spread snowfall from northwest to southeast across the area. This was an early season storm, and was the first significant winter storm for many locations, including some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. Gusty winds also accompanied the cold front and generated areas of blowing and drifting snow which caused hazardous travel conditions, especially over the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 12 inches occurred across the area while wind gusts up to 30 mph caused areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold Pacific storm system dropped down into the Great Basin and then tracked through the Rocky Mountain region, pushing a strong cold front that spread snowfall from northwest to southeast across the area. This was an early season storm, and was the first significant winter storm for many locations, including some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. Gusty winds also accompanied the cold front and generated areas of blowing and drifting snow which caused hazardous travel conditions, especially over the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the area ranged from 9 to 22 inches, with locally higher amounts of up to 26 inches measured at Schofield Pass. Wind gusts up to 30 mph caused areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching the area from the northwest generated strong and gusty southerly winds ahead of an associated cold front. The strong winds caused some damage in the Grand Junction area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly surface winds blew a large tree branch down on top of a power transformer.  The branch severed a local power line and destroyed the transformer, causing a large power outage in the Redlands area of the Grand Valley.  Additionally, several radio stations in other areas of Grand Junction went off the air due to power outages.  Although measured wind gusts only reached 54 mph in the Grand Valley, it is estimated that there were some stronger gusts where wind measurements were not available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed east of the Interstate 25 corridor and in the Northeast Plains.  Most of the storms produced damaging straightline winds.  Hail, very heavy rainfall and one tornado were also reported.  Most of the damage occurred in the city of Sterling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds downed a light pole in Sterling, along with several trees.  A roof was also torn off an apartment building.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of two Pacific storms tracked across the Rocky Mountain region bringing widespread winter conditions to western Colorado. The first system dragged a cold front across the state and spread snowfall across the western Colorado mountains and valleys of northwest Colorado. The second low pressure system dropped down into the Great Basin region and formed a closed low pressure system near the four corner region. This continued mountain snowfall across the central and southern Colorado mountains, and brought significant snow to some of the southern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 18 inches occurred across the area with wind gusts up to 40 mph producing blowing and drifting snow. Travel conditions became hazardous, especially over the major mountain passes. Chain law restrictions were put into effect over Vail Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold Pacific storm system dropped down into the Great Basin and then tracked through the Rocky Mountain region, pushing a strong cold front that spread snowfall from northwest to southeast across the area. This was an early season storm, and was the first significant winter storm for many locations, including some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. Gusty winds also accompanied the cold front and generated areas of blowing and drifting snow which caused hazardous travel conditions, especially over the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 12 inches occurred across the area. Locally higher amounts included 16 inches at the Bison Lake SNOTEL site.

Some snow amounts include...around a foot near the Royal Gorge (Fremont County)...Monarch Pass (Chaffee County) and Rosita (Custer County)...14 to 16 inches around Woodland Park (Teller County)...Rye and Beulah (Pueblo County)...Salida (Chaffee County)...Cuchara and La Veta (Huerfano County)...17 to 19 inches near the Spanish Peaks (Huerfano County)...Maysville (Chaffee County) and Mounument (El Paso County)...22 to 25 inches just northwest of Buena Vista and southwest of Nathrop (Chaffee County)...northeast of Florissant (Teller County)...and the summit of Wolf Creek Pass (Mineral County).

A significant early season snowstorm occurred across north central and northeast Colorado.  Storm totals ranged from 20 to 46 inches in the Front Range Mountains and Foothills, 12 to 26 inches across the Urban Corridor, and 4 to 12 inches over the Northeast Plains of Colorado.   The combination of a deep northeasterly upslope flow coupled with abundant moisture and lift with the developing storm system produced an extended period of moderate to heavy snowfall.  The heavy wet snowfall accumulated on trees and resulted in broken branches and scattered electrical outages.  ||Once the storm spread into the Northeast Plains, the strong winds gusting to 45 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions east of Denver.  Interstates 70 and 76 were closed east of Denver.  Numerous other roads and highways were shut down including State Highways 34 and 36.  The Red Cross set up numerous emergency shelters for stranded travelers.  The blowing snow at Denver International Airport forced the cancellation of hundreds of flights.   Schools throughout the region were also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 5 to 7 inches around Sterling.  North to northwest winds gusting up to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.  The drifts were up to 3 feet in depth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 5 inches, 18 miles southwest of Fleming.  North to northwest winds gusted up to 48 mph at Akron Airport.

Nine to 11 inches of snow occurred around Boncarbo in Las Animas County and Walsenburg in Huerfano County.  Around a foot of snow was measured around Trinidad (Las Animas County)...Beulah (Pueblo County)...La Veta and the Spanish Peaks (Huerfano County)...and Wetmore (Custer County).  Finally...15 to 17 inches of snow covered the communities of Westcliffe (Custer County)...Rye (Pueblo County) and Cuchara (Huerfano County).

Heavy snow developed along the Palmer Divide and eastern Colorado as a storm system tracked across southern Colorado and northern New Mexico.  The heaviest snow fell to the south and east of Denver.  Storm totals included:  15 inches, 15 miles north of Elizabeth, 14 inches at Agate, 13 inches, 14 miles west-southwest of Agate; 11 inches, 2 miles east of Elizabeth; 11 inches at Hugo, 9 inches, 5 miles south-southwest of Arapahoe Park; 8 inches, 4 miles east of Akron, Highlands Ranch, 5 miles south-southeast of Sedalia;  7 inches near Antero Junction and Karval, 6.5 inches, 8 miles southwest of Bennett and 6 inches in Parker.  Storm totals elsewhere range from 3 to 5 inches.  In Karval, in southern Lincoln County, the combination of ice and wind knocked the power out in the area.  Approximately 70 power poles were knocked down during the storm.

Heavy snow developed along the Palmer Divide and eastern Colorado as a storm system tracked across southern Colorado and northern New Mexico.  The heaviest snow fell to the south and east of Denver.  Storm totals included:  15 inches, 15 miles north of Elizabeth, 14 inches at Agate, 13 inches, 14 miles west-southwest of Agate; 11 inches, 2 miles east of Elizabeth; 11 inches at Hugo, 9 inches, 5 miles south-southwest of Arapahoe Park; 8 inches, 4 miles east of Akron, Highlands Ranch, 5 miles south-southeast of Sedalia;  7 inches near Antero Junction and Karval, 6.5 inches, 8 miles southwest of Bennett and 6 inches in Parker.  Storm totals elsewhere range from 3 to 5 inches.  In Karval, in southern Lincoln County, the combination of ice and wind knocked the power out in the area.  Approximately 70 power poles were knocked down during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and stable airmass over the area resulted in widespread dense fog formation shortly after sunset, which lasted through the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and saturated airmass in the wake of a departing winter storm system was in place over the San Juan River Basin. This allowed widespread dense fog to develop shortly after sunset from areas near Pagosa Springs southward to the New Mexico border. Visibilities in some areas were reduced to only 100 to 200 feet along highway 84.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold Pacific storm system dropped down into the Great Basin and then tracked through the Rocky Mountain region, pushing a strong cold front that spread snowfall from northwest to southeast across the area. This was an early season storm, and was the first significant winter storm for many locations, including some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. Gusty winds also accompanied the cold front and generated areas of blowing and drifting snow which caused hazardous travel conditions, especially over the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the first winter storm of the season to affect the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River basins. Snowfall of 3 to 8 inches occurred across the area, with wind gusts up to 30 mph causing blowing and drifting snow. Several power outages were caused by heavy snow laden tree branches falling on top of power lines, forcing them all the way to the ground in some areas. A few hundred customers experienced power outages in the towns of Montrose and Paonia, with some of the outages lasting up to 4 and a half hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: North of Eleven Mile State Park, the tornado destroyed to small sheds and a camper, while a steel framed barn suffered major damage.  Two homes received minor damage including:  broken windows, cracked siding, lost shingles and a collapsed deck awning.  At another property northeast of Lake George, another camper was flipped and destroyed.  Minor damage was also observed with signs knocked down along State Highway 24.  Between Eleven Mile Reservoir and Lake George, there were numerous areas of significant tree damage.  Large ponderosa pines, two feet in diameter, were uprooted and snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The potent combination of a Canadian cold front, mid level instability and a strong upper level jetstream produced bands of heavy snow across the northern tier of counties in Northeast Colorado.  The heavy bands of snow stretched from the foothills of Larimer and Boulder Counties into Weld, Logan, Sedwick and Phillips Counties.  The heavy snow reduced visibilities to less than a quarter mile at times.  Storm totals included:  17 inches at Virginia Dale, 11 inches at Wellington, 10 inches, 21 miles north of New Raymer; 8 inches at Fleming, 7 inches at Crook and Hereford, 6 inches in Boulder, 3 miles west of Jamestown and Peetz.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early season snowstorm occurred across north central and northeast Colorado.  Storm totals ranged from 20 to 46 inches in the Front Range Mountains and Foothills, 12 to 26 inches across the Urban Corridor, and 4 to 12 inches over the Northeast Plains of Colorado.   The combination of a deep northeasterly upslope flow coupled with abundant moisture and lift with the developing storm system produced an extended period of moderate to heavy snowfall.  The heavy wet snowfall accumulated on trees and resulted in broken branches and scattered electrical outages.  ||Once the storm spread into the Northeast Plains, the strong winds gusting to 45 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions east of Denver.  Interstates 70 and 76 were closed east of Denver.  Numerous other roads and highways were shut down including State Highways 34 and 36.  The Red Cross set up numerous emergency shelters for stranded travelers.  The blowing snow at Denver International Airport forced the cancellation of hundreds of flights.   Schools throughout the region were also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:   25 inches in Highlands Ranch, 17 inches in Westminster, 15 inches in Littleton, 14 inches in Englewood, 13.5 inches, 6 miles north of Thornton; 11 inches, 4 miles south of Denver; 10.5 inches, 2 miles north of Cherry Hills Village and 13.3 inches at Denver International Airport.

Storm totals ranged from 20 to 46 inches in the Front Range Mountains and Foothills, 12 to 26 inches across the Urban Corridor, and 4 to 12 inches over the Northeast Plains of Colorado.   The combination of a deep northeasterly upslope flow coupled with abundant moisture and lift with the developing storm system produced an extended period of moderate to heavy snowfall. Interstates 70 and 76 were closed east of Denver.  Numerous other roads and highways were shut down including State Highways 34 and 36.  The Red Cross set up numerous emergency shelters for stranded travelers.  The blowing snow at Denver International Airport forced the cancellation of hundreds of flights.   Schools throughout the region were also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 17.5 inches at C.S.U. in Fort Collins and 14.5 inches, 7 miles west-northwest of Loveland.
